Applications of
Conformal Mappings

Hydrodynamics

If we have a (steady-state) incompressible, nonviscous fluid, we are interested in finding its velocity field @@math:5:31d61b@@ From vector analysis we know that 'incompressible' means that the divergence @@math:8:f4e8f0@@ (We say @@math:9:ac2b27@@ is divergence free.) We assume that @@math:10:b7b582@@ is also a potential flow and hence is circulation free; that is @@math:11:f31695@@ for some @@math:12:ed00fa@@ called the velocity potential. Thus @@math:13:8ff67f@@ is harmonic because @@math:6:1aef21@@ Thus when we solve for @@math:14:3eb543@@ we can obtain @@math:15:9e65ef@@ by taking @@math:16:7cb5d3@@. That is @@math:3:bf3f34@@

The conjugate @@math:17:a85df0@@ of the harmonic function @@math:18:6cb994@@ (which will exist on any simple connected region) is called the stream function, and the analytic function @@math:7:055746@@ is called the complex potential.

The stream function must satisfy @@math:4:9c3354@@ Finally, lines of constant @@math:19:ce35be@@ have @@math:20:81d5c0@@ as their tangents, so lines of constant @@math:21:da0f6e@@ may be interpreted as the lines along which particles of fluid move; hence the name stream function.
